channel.title: Comments for page "SCP-2602 Photographic Evidence"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "SCP-2602 Photographic Evidence"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unfortunately, I made the mistake of reading this post, which appears to describe a library, before I read the mainline article, which formerly was a library. I was very confused, until I realized that 2602 was a library.

+1 library because library library, library.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool. At first when reading the article I interpreted it as sorta being like a front for a mafia-like organization that stuck to become anomalous. Like people were so overtly insistent on "nobody should think this place was anything other than a library, see?" that nobody can even mention it now without bringing it up. But here it's somewhat different, you get the idea it's maybe like a living building that consumes people, or is used by creatures or inhabitants that do sinister things and the cover of having been a library comes from them being like aliens who are bad at keeping up a cover and can only do so very bluntly. Either way, fun to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's sad that no one notices samothrace on the filing cabinets. Ay btw you hear about that one time down in samothrace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a bit different than what I expected a former library to look like (which I suspect has to do with the period during which I originally suspected that it used to be a library), but it's still a quite lovely collection of images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A former library.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The neatly stacked bones have me confused even more than I already was by 2602. What was it after it was a library?

item.title: Re: Minor rewording?
item.content:encoded: Fixed! I wrote the captions well after I did the pictures, and kinda quickly.

item.title: Minor rewording?
item.content:encoded:
The phrase "sadly typical" is used in two captions in a row, which is a shame given that former libraries are commonly associated with interestingly diverse writing. Perhaps consider changing to "unfortunately common" or some such?

Very nice addition, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dont see anything strange about this although to the best of my knowledge former libaries usually have carrion feeders ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A library, obviously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Analysis: It's perfectly normal and arguably quite convenient for circulation carts to levitate in the presence of Dewey radiation typical of former libraries.

I've decided this is my favorite one.
Also, still want to know the hell was this former library.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted specifically for "sentient, edible life", but this whole thing was great. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did I not notice that before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… >.> …..<.<….

+ show block
– hide block

It's a reptile eye.

I originally had a bunch of other stuff glommed onto the dustcover, but a friend suggested the minimalist option.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What paper?

Edit: If you mean the SCP-2140-1-F(L) instance in image -116, the writing system is High Daeva Thaumaturgical-B.

However I am talking about image -117, in which no paper seems to be visible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The paper at the top doesn't look like it's in any writing system I'm aware of, but I dunno if I'm just stupid or if that's the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there anything in the image of the microfiche reader that I am missing? Or is that the one normal picture in the bunch?

ETA: Is that a limpet or some other mollusk on the inside of the dustcover?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this to an extent typical of a former library worker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed, it is. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is all sorts of freaky and weird and I love it. Just the strangeness I would hope for when viewing images of a former library.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Roger. I'll ask the tech team what I can do about that.

Edit: My thanks to anqxyr. Turns out I was using the total wrong format. It should be much more loadable now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pictures are too big, not loading on my terribly slow internet connection. I vaguely recall them from when Scan added them to the main page but I'd rather see the content+captions as you intend them to be seen here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this in December, love it now. Since it's sitting at +9, I've gone ahead and added a link to it in SCP-2602; up to you whether you want to categorize this as a supplement or a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to finally see this up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1'd for all these files…that used to be in a library.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huzzah!

So, most of this was my 2016 Holiday Exchange gift for Communism Will Win. I did a couple more on recommendation from sirpudding, and the format and concept was suggested by CWW.

I don't super consider this canon for SCP-2602, I was just kind of having fun throwing everything at it.

Image sources include:
All library images: Myself and Casey Smith, CC BY-SA 3.0. Some detail for SCP-2140-1-L(F) is from a CC font by Juan Carlos Porcel, concept used with permission from sirpudding. Other details are CC0 from pixabay.

The exceptions:
Quiet Study includes Catacombs of Paris by Michael Reeve, CC BY-SA 3.0 on wikimedia commons.
Exbibliothetic Possession is mostly Reactor nuclear experimental RA-6 (Republica Argentina 6), en marcha, Centro Atomico Bariloche by Pieck Darío, CC BY-SA 3.0 on wikimedia commons.
